name: Roast Turkey with Chestnut Stuffing
recipeInstructions:
To peel chestnuts, make a small cut on the flat side of each chestnut, cutting all the way through the outer skin. Place chestnuts in a medium-size saucepan, cover with water, and bring to a boil over high heat. Reduce heat to low. Remove several chestnuts from the water and peel them with a sharp paring knife, removing both the outer peel and the dark inner skin attached to the meat. If chestnuts cool before peeling, place them back in hot water.
Remove giblets and neck from the turkey; set aside. Rinse the turkey well inside and out until water runs clear. Pat it thoroughly dry. Carefully separate the skin from the breast, avoiding holes in the skin. Spread butter on the meat.
Bring water to a boil in a vegetable steamer over high heat. Add chestnuts, cover, and steam until tender (about 30 minutes). Remove steamer basket from heat.
Gently break chestnuts into bite-size pieces in a bowl. Stir in pork, shallots, herbs, salt, pepper, garlic, and eggs. Mix well after each addition. Chop giblets and add to the mixture; mix thoroughly.
Pinch off a teaspoon of the mixture and cook in a small skillet over medium heat until cooked through. Taste and adjust seasoning as needed.
Fill turkey with stuffing, not tightly packed. Tuck legs slightly higher than breast to allow juices to run back into meat. Cover turkey loosely if breast browns too quickly. Roast at 350°F for golden color, basting every 30 minutes. Add onions and carrots after 2 hours, spacing evenly around turkey. Turkey is done when skin is golden, thigh tender (press), and leg joints move easily.
Let turkey rest at least 30 minutes or up to 45 minutes before carving. Prop legs higher than breast for juices to return. Reduce cooking juices slightly over medium-high heat for 2 minutes, scraping brown bits. Season and strain juices into a gravy boat.
Serve stuffing in serving dish with gravy boat; pass gravy as needed.
Yield: 8-10 servings
The stuffing is versatile for other poultry dishes and can be cooked separately, taking slightly less time for the turkey to cook. Also good with red Burgundy or Volnay.
